title: Kim Gehrig

infobox.value: is an Australian [director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director") whose body of work spans [television shows](/wiki/Television_show "Television show"), commercials, music videos, [documentaries](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), short films and branded entertainment. Gehrig directed "The Woman Who Ate Photographs", an episode of [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") series, "Roar", starring [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"). Her notable commercial works include Run This Town and Stay for [Rihanna's](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") [Halftime Show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII_halftime_show "Super Bowl LVII halftime show"), The Greatest for Apple, Viva La Vulva for Libresse, Life Needs Truth for The New York Times, the [This Girl Can](/wiki/This_Girl_Can "This Girl Can") campaign, and work for many more for brands including Airbnb, Google, Lyft, Nike, [IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA"), [GAP](/wiki/Gap_Inc. "Gap Inc."), and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"). In music videos, Gehrig has collaborated with artists such as [Chaka Khan](/wiki/Chaka_Khan "Chaka Khan"), [Brittany Howard](/wiki/Brittany_Howard "Brittany Howard"), [Wiley](/wiki/Wiley_(musician) "Wiley (musician)"), [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris"), and [Basement Jaxx](/wiki/Basement_Jaxx "Basement Jaxx"). In 2023, Gehrig became the 2nd woman of all time to win the [DGA](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America "Directors Guild of America") Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement in Commercials and her films have won a plethora of awards including [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity"), D&AD pencils, BTAA Arrows and [UKMVA](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards")'s.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
[Thinkbox](/wiki/Thinkbox "Thinkbox") October award for 'You are Powerful' (Amnesty International).[[31]](#cite_note-31)
Best Urban Video for Wiley's 'Cash in My Pocket' at the [UK Music Video Awards](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards").[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11)

Broadcast Award by [Young Director Awards](/w/index.php?title=Young_Director_Awards&action=edit&redlink=1 "Young Director Awards (page does not exist)") for 'You are Powerful' (Amnesty International).[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5)
Top director of the year by [Campaign](/wiki/Campaign_(magazine) "Campaign (magazine)").[[32]](#cite_note-32)
Three silver [British Arrows](/wiki/British_Arrows "British Arrows") for 'This Girl Can' by Sport England.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Gehrig directed "The Woman Who Ate Photographs", an episode of [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") series, "Roar", starring [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"). "Gap Inc."), and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"). In music videos, Gehrig has collaborated with artists such as [Chaka Khan](/wiki/Chaka_Khan "Chaka Khan"), [Brittany Howard](/wiki/Brittany_Howard "Brittany Howard"), [Wiley](/wiki/Wiley_(musician) "Wiley (musician)"), [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris"), and [Basement Jaxx](/wiki/Basement_Jaxx "Basement Jaxx"). In 2023, Gehrig became the 2nd woman of all time to win the [DGA](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America "Directors Guild of America") Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement in Commercials and her films have won a plethora of awards including [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity"), D&AD pencils, BTAA Arrows and [UKMVA](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards")'s.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian director

| Kim Gehrig | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia |
| Occupation | Film director |
| Children | 2 |

**Kim Gehrig** is an Australian [director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director") whose body of work spans [television shows](/wiki/Television_show "Television show"), commercials, music videos, [documentaries](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), short films and branded entertainment. Gehrig directed "The Woman Who Ate Photographs", an episode of [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") series, "Roar", starring [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"). Her notable commercial works include Run This Town and Stay for [Rihanna's](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") [Halftime Show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII_halftime_show "Super Bowl LVII halftime show"), The Greatest for Apple, Viva La Vulva for Libresse, Life Needs Truth for The New York Times, the [This Girl Can](/wiki/This_Girl_Can "This Girl Can") campaign, and work for many more for brands including Airbnb, Google, Lyft, Nike, [IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA"), [GAP](/wiki/Gap_Inc. "Gap Inc."), and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"). In music videos, Gehrig has collaborated with artists such as [Chaka Khan](/wiki/Chaka_Khan "Chaka Khan"), [Brittany Howard](/wiki/Brittany_Howard "Brittany Howard"), [Wiley](/wiki/Wiley_(musician) "Wiley (musician)"), [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris"), and [Basement Jaxx](/wiki/Basement_Jaxx "Basement Jaxx"). In 2023, Gehrig became the 2nd woman of all time to win the [DGA](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America "Directors Guild of America") Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement in Commercials and her films have won a plethora of awards including [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity"), D&AD pencils, BTAA Arrows and [UKMVA](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards")'s.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

One of her first major works as a director was the 'You are Powerful' video for [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), released on the 60th anniversary of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) For this she won a Silver Lion at [Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity") 2010 and a broadcast award by the Young Director Awards.[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5)

Gehrig is represented by Somesuch, who were awarded [Advertising Age](/wiki/Advertising_Age "Advertising Age")'s 2018 Production Company of the Year.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Music Videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Music Videos")]

Gehrig has directed numerous music videos in addition to her advertising work. She won 'Best Urban Music Video' at the 2009 [UK Music Video Awards](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards") for [Wiley 's](/wiki/Wiley_(musician) "Wiley (musician)") [*'Cash in My Pocket'*](/wiki/Cash_in_My_Pocket "Cash in My Pocket"), and was nominated in 2018 for her music video for [Chaka Khan](/wiki/Chaka_Khan "Chaka Khan")'s *'Like Suga'*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Television")]

Gehrig directed "The Woman Who Ate Photographs", an episode of [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") series, "Roar", starring [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman") written by [Liz Flahive](/wiki/Liz_Flahive "Liz Flahive") and [Carly Mensch](/wiki/Carly_Mensch "Carly Mensch").

### The Greatest (Apple)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Greatest (Apple)")]

Her recent film for [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), 'The Greatest,' challenged common stereotypes about people with disabilities while highlighting accessibility features available on the iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Watch. The 2-minute film was made with the partnership and feedback of the disability community, both in front of and behind the camera. The lyrics of the film's soundtrack are based on speeches by [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali"), who became an advocate for disability after developing [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"). The Australian group [Spinifex Gum](/wiki/Spinifex_Gum "Spinifex Gum") sang the song, which was then remixed by [Cola Boyy](/wiki/Cola_Boyy "Cola Boyy") along with improvised piano by [Matthew Whitaker](/wiki/Matthew_Whitaker_(pianist) "Matthew Whitaker (pianist)"). Apple also released an audio-described version of the film to bring awareness to the needs of blind and low-vision audiences.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Viva la Vulva (Libresse)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Viva la Vulva (Libresse)")]

Gehrig directed the award-winning 'Viva la Vulva' advert for [Libresse](/wiki/Libresse "Libresse"), a celebration of the [vagina](/wiki/Vagina "Vagina") and women body confidence. The video contains many items from normal life which resemble vaginas as well as puppetry and outfits resembling genitalia. It was set to the music of [Camille Yarbrough](/wiki/Camille_Yarbrough "Camille Yarbrough")'s '*Take Yo' Praise'.*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### This Girl Can (Sport England)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: This Girl Can (Sport England)")]

Gehrig directed the multi award-winning advert for the [This Girl Can](/wiki/This_Girl_Can "This Girl Can") campaign by [Sport England](/wiki/Sport_England "Sport England") to encourage more women to take part in sport. The video features personal and unfiltered clips of women exercising as they would be normally.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The advert was praised for its bold and empowering message, winning awards including a Glass Lion for Good at [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity") 2015.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)[[18]](#cite_note-:2-18) Sport England estimated that an additional 2.8 million women decided to take up a sport after the advert's release.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) The first advert was followed by 'Phenomenal Women', showcasing women from all walks of life engaging in sport set to [Maya Angelou](/wiki/Maya_Angelou "Maya Angelou")'s recital of [*Phenomenal Woman*](/wiki/Phenomenal_Woman:_Four_Poems_Celebrating_Women "Phenomenal Woman: Four Poems Celebrating Women").[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) Gehrig stated in 2018 that 'This Girl Can' is the video she is most proud of.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Man on The Moon (John Lewis)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Man on The Moon (John Lewis)")]

Later in 2015 Gehrig premiered the first [John Lewis](/wiki/John_Lewis_%26_Partners "John Lewis & Partners") Christmas advert to be directed by a woman; 'Man on The Moon'. The advert constituted part of John Lewis' partnership with [Age UK](/wiki/Age_UK "Age UK") to raise awareness of [loneliness](/wiki/Loneliness "Loneliness") in the [elderly](/wiki/Elderly "Elderly"), showing a lonely old man living on the moon being sent a gift by a young girl on earth. The video was set to a cover of [Oasis'](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") [*'Half the World Away*'](/wiki/Half_the_World_Away "Half the World Away") by [Aurora.](/wiki/Aurora_(singer) "Aurora (singer)")[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### The Best Men Can Be (Gillette)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Best Men Can Be (Gillette)")]

Gehrig again rose to prominence in 2019 for '[The Best Men Can Be](/wiki/The_Best_Men_Can_Be "The Best Men Can Be")' advertising video by razor company [Gillette](/wiki/Gillette "Gillette"), which challenges its earlier tagline of "The Best a Man Can Get" and the effects of [toxic masculinity](/wiki/Toxic_masculinity "Toxic masculinity"). The advert was praised by some for its message against the traditional concepts of how men should be, and how this impacts [mental health](/wiki/Mental_health "Mental health") as well as women.[[23]](#cite_note-:7-23)[[24]](#cite_note-:8-24) However, the advert was criticised by others for its negative portrayal of men. Multiple commentators, such as [Piers Morgan](/wiki/Piers_Morgan "Piers Morgan"), were incensed by the campaign and vowed to boycott the company.[[25]](#cite_note-:3-25)[[23]](#cite_note-:7-23)[[24]](#cite_note-:8-24) *[Marketing Week](/wiki/Marketing_Week "Marketing Week")* saw the backlash towards the ad as backfiring on Gillette following declining sales metrics for the brand that led to a $8bn writedown on the *carrying value of Gillette’s goodwill*.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Gehrig responded to some of the criticism by saying: "The project had actually been conceived, written and edited by men. But because I'm a female director, it fitted neatly into a troll's narrative."[[29]](#cite_note-:9-29)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Gehrig was born in [Sydney, Australia](/wiki/Sydney,_Australia "Sydney, Australia"), and moved to [London](/wiki/London "London") to study at [Central Saint Martins](/wiki/Central_Saint_Martins "Central Saint Martins"), [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London"). The first eight years of her career were spent as a Creative at advertising agency Mother.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Gehrig has two daughters, and currently lives in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[29]](#cite_note-:9-29)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Awards")]

* **2008** [Thinkbox](/wiki/Thinkbox "Thinkbox") October award for 'You are Powerful' (Amnesty International).[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* **2009** Best Urban Video for Wiley's 'Cash in My Pocket' at the [UK Music Video Awards](/wiki/UK_Music_Video_Awards "UK Music Video Awards").[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11)
* **2010** Broadcast Award by [Young Director Awards](/w/index.php?title=Young_Director_Awards&action=edit&redlink=1 "Young Director Awards (page does not exist)") for 'You are Powerful' (Amnesty International).[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5)
* **2010** Silver Lion by [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity") for 'You are Powerful' (Amnesty International)[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5)
* **2015** Top director of the year by [Campaign](/wiki/Campaign_(magazine) "Campaign (magazine)").[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* **2015** Most creative people of the year by [Fast Company](/wiki/Fast_Company "Fast Company").[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* **2015** Grand Prix at the Glass Lion for Good Awards [Cannes Lions](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity") for 'This Girl Can' by Sport England.[[18]](#cite_note-:2-18)
* **2015** Twelve total awards, including Gold for Best Direction - Single at [Creative Circle](/wiki/Creative_Circle "Creative Circle") for 'This Girl Can' by Sport England.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* **2016** Three silver [British Arrows](/wiki/British_Arrows "British Arrows") for 'This Girl Can' by Sport England.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* **2016** Graphite and White [D&ad](/wiki/D%26ad "D&ad") Pencils for 'This Girl Can' by Sport England.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* **2016** Two silver and one bronze British Arrow for 'Man on the Moon' by John Lewis.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* **2017** Wood D&ad Pencil for 'This Girl Can: Phenomenal Women' by Sport England.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* **2018** Most creative advertising campaign of the year by [Advertising Age](/wiki/Advertising_Age "Advertising Age") for 'Viva la Vulva' by [Libresse](/wiki/Libresse "Libresse").[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* **2018** Advert of the year by [Creative Review](/wiki/Creative_Review "Creative Review") for 'Viva la Vulva' by Libresse.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* **2019** Black D&AD Pencil for 'Viva La Vulva' by Libresse.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* **2021** Adage[[42]](#cite_note-42) Director of the Year
* **2022** [Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement in Commercials](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_%E2%80%93_Commercials "Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directing – Commercials") for directing Apple's *Accessibility* and *Run Baby Run*
* **2024** [Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement in Commercials](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_%E2%80%93_Commercials "Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directing – Commercials") for directing *Run This Town*, Apple Music – Apple (Client Direct) and *The Travelers*, Expedia – Wieden & Kennedy[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kim_Gehrig&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Framke, Caroline (11 April 2022). ["'Roar' Throws Nicole Kidman, Issa Rae, Alison Brie and More Into a Mixed Bag of Feminist Fables: TV Review"](https://variety.com/2022/tv/reviews/roar-review-apple-nicole-kidman-1235228844/). *Variety*. Retrieved 20 November 2025.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Elmes, John. ["Kim Gehrig: Roar shines spotlight on unseen female characters"](https://www.broadcastnow.co.uk/international/kim-gehrig-roar-shines-spotlight-on-unseen-female-characters/5169610.article). *Broadcast*. Retrieved 20 November 2025.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** ["Amnesty You Are Powerful"](http://theinspirationroom.com/daily/2008/amnesty-you-are-powerful/). *The Inspiration Room*. 17 December 2008. Retrieved 1 February 2019.
